channel.title: Rpl on Docs
channel.description: Recent content in Rpl on Docs

item.title: Chapter 2, Part 5 – RPL Protocol
item.description:
What Is RPL?

RPL (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks) is a lightweight distance-vector routing protocol designed specifically for IoT devices — constrained sensors with limited memory, processing power, and battery.

A lossy network is one with a high packet drop rate, which is the norm in wireless sensor environments. RPL is built to handle this gracefully.

RPL’s core mechanism: it builds a DODAG (Destination-Oriented Directed Acyclic Graph) — a tree-like, loop-free topology rooted at a single designated root node.

item.title: Chapter 2, Part 6 – AI-Enhanced RPL Routing
item.description:
The Limitation of Traditional RPL

Traditional RPL selects parents using a fixed Objective Function with static weights:

Cost = W1*(Delay) + W2*(ETX) + W3*(Energy)

The weights are set at design time and never change. The network is static by assumption. But real IoT networks are not static — traffic patterns shift, nodes drain their batteries, links degrade, and topology changes. Fixed weights cannot adapt to any of this.

AI makes RPL adaptive instead of static.
